summary:
The options included various means by which our bodies can shed excess heat physiologically through evaporation via sweating, increased surface area for radiation with vessel dilation, and involuntary shivers generating internal heat rather than losing external thermal load directly. Posture adjustment was noted yet deemed insufficiently specific or impactful relative to these core processes designed explicitly around cooling down rapidly when needed most acutely during overheating events. Thus, changing one’s position fails to meet criteria defining principal avenues of thermoregulatory dissipation seen across species adapted towards maintaining homeostasis under diverse environmental conditions encountered regularly throughout life spans spanning decades typically experienced today globally among human populations inhabiting varied climates ranging widely between extremes found at poles versus equator regions worldwide currently documented scientifically using modern climatological data collection techniques available now after centuries since initial observations began being recorded systematically starting roughly ~500 years ago historically speaking broadly covering all continents except Antarctica where no indigenous permanent settlements exist due primarily logistical challenges associated establishing sustainable communities capable surviving long term without significant technological support infrastructure already present locally prior migrations occurred naturally over millennia before recent rapid global population growth trends observed recently especially within last few hundred years driven largely economic development advancements enabled widespread adoption agricultural practices industrial revolution innovations etc... To reiterate succinctly though just focusing strictly clinically here:

The correct non-method-of-heat-loss option must be C) Altering postural positioning because while changes do influence local circulations minimally they don't constitute an efficient nor reliable
